engineering.translation: A certain class of speech circulates through every engineering organisation, and on first hearing it sounds beautiful — the diction is polished, the syntax cleanly arranged, the charts blooming with growth. Yet examined carefully, it contains nothing beyond a single bounded set of fruits: shipping velocity, feature-count, dashboard-green, weekly active users. The verse names the speakers undiscerning, and names their characteristic claim as *there is nothing else*. This teaching is not anti-metric — it identifies the exclusive fixation that treats one bounded number as the entire interpretive frame. Consider two operators. The first reads conversion alongside on-call burden, error budget, and downstream wellbeing. The second treats only p99 latency, or only retention, as the thing that genuinely matters. The second is the operator the verse names: devoted to one segment of the available signal as though it constituted the whole. Krishna does not reject the ritual portion of scripture; he rejects the reading that treats that portion as the totality.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A growth-engineering team has settled into a comfortable cadence. Every Monday opens with three slides. The first is a conversion-rate dashboard piped from Mixpanel through Datadog. The second is a demo-week feature count drawn from the GitHub release tag. The third is the launch-tweet engagement numbers supplied by the marketing partner. The meeting feels productive. The chart rendering is clean, the slides are polished, the team is genuinely skilled at this. Eighteen months pass. On-call burden has roughly doubled. An early customer-success metric has decoupled from conversion: paying customers convert, then churn within ninety days. Three engineers have quietly opened LinkedIn. None of those signals appears in the standing review. When a senior architect mentions the on-call PagerDuty trend at the end of the agenda, the response is polite redirection — we will revisit that next quarter; the launch is the priority right now. The original numbers are accurate. The missing ones are equally accurate. The pathology lies not in the chosen metrics. It lies in the implicit clause *there is nothing else*. The team has become devoted to a beautiful portion of the available evidence as though it were the totality of evidence. The standing review's flowery speech is not a moral failure. It is a discrimination failure — undiscerning of what the review has quietly scoped out.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to justify dismissing all metric-driven culture, treating dashboards as 'flowery' and intuition as 'real.' That is the inverted error. The verse criticises exclusive fixation, not measurement. A team that reads conversion-rate, p99, error-budget, on-call burden, customer-success curves, and operator-wellbeing signals together is doing the discriminating work the verse asks for. A team that reads only retention or only latency is the undiscerning operator the verse names. The diagnostic is the implicit 'nothing else' clause — not the choice of any one metric. If the team can articulate which signals their current frame excludes and why, this verse does not apply yet.
engineering.counter_example: When a particular metric IS load-bearing for the next-quarter decision — cash runway, regulatory deadline, a specific customer SLA — focused attention on that metric is correct, not pathological. The verse does not bless attention-spread for its own sake. The pathology is the implicit closure ('there is nothing else'), not the focused attention. A team that says 'this quarter, we are optimising the checkout funnel because runway requires it; here are the signals we are deliberately deprioritising and the date we will revisit them' has done the discrimination this verse calls for. The pathology is not attentiveness — it is attentiveness coupled with closure to everything else.
engineering.implication: Audit your standing review. Name three signals it excludes and the implicit 'there is nothing else' clause those exclusions express. The pathology is rarely loud; it is the polished review whose beauty depends on the missing slides. The corrective is not to add every signal — it is to surface the closure clause and let it be examined.
engineering.quotable_line: The standing review's polish often depends on the slides it doesn't have; the missing slides are the verse's flowery speech in negative.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.42 is the first half of the famously sharp critique of veda-vāda-rata. CRITICAL: the engineering layer must NOT generalise to anti-metrics. Per Shankara and Ramanuja, the critique is of fixation on a portion of the Vedas (the kāmya-karma) treated as the totality. Engineering layer therefore anchors on exclusive metric-fixation: a polished growth-engineering standing review that treats conversion-rate / feature-count / engagement as 'there is nothing else.' Concrete scenario uses Mixpanel + demo-week + launch-tweet trio with hidden on-call burden, customer-success decoupling, and engineer job-searching as the excluded signals. Falsifiability handles the inverse error (dismissing all metric culture as flowery). Counter-example carves out legitimate focused attention (load-bearing metric for next quarter) — the pathology is the closure clause, not the focus. Tags: vanity-metrics, feedback-loops, metrics-discipline. Voice held to chapter 2 didactic-corrective register.

v2 editorial pass: replaced technical Sanskrit terms in engineering register with English equivalents (avipaścitaḥ → undiscerning; veda-vāda-rata → the pattern named; kāmya-karma → ritual portion); split compound sentences (Monday slide-trio split into three sentences); added prose connectives. The closure-clause nuance ('there is nothing else') is preserved as the diagnostic. Doctrinal content unchanged.
